advantages: Non-saturating for x > 0: gradient stays at 1 on the active branch, avoiding sigmoid-style vanishing gradients in deep positive regions.
Computationally cheap: max(0, x) is faster than exponentials—important for large batches and inference on GPUs/edge devices.
Induces sparsity: zeroing negative activations reduces effective connectivity and can improve generalization and interpretability.
Simple to implement and tune: no extra hyperparameters for standard ReLU beyond placement in the architecture.
disadvantages: Dying ReLU problem: neurons stuck at zero output receive no gradient and may never contribute again.
Not zero-centered: outputs are always ≥ 0, which can cause zig-zagging updates compared to tanh or batch-normalized activations.
Unbounded above: large positive pre-activations pass through unchanged, which can contribute to exploding activations without normalization.
Not differentiable at x = 0: optimization uses subgradients; rare corner cases at exactly zero are handled differently across frameworks.
